questions.text: How much net gain of G3P is obtained after one calvin cycle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is incorrect. While three molecules of CO2 are fixed in one Calvin cycle, the net gain of G3P is not three. Most of the G3P produced is used to regenerate RuBP, leaving only a net gain of one G3P.
It is incorrect. Six G3P molecules are produced initially in the Calvin cycle, but only one is available as a net gain after regeneration of RuBP, the molecule that accepts CO2.
It is incorrect.Two G3P molecules are not the net gain per Calvin cycle. While multiple G3P molecules are produced, only one is available as a net gain after the necessary regeneration of RuBP.

For every three molecules of CO2 that enter the Calvin cycle, one molecule of G3P is produced. However, it's important to note that only one out of every six molecules of G3P produced during the Calvin cycle is used to build glucose and other carbohydrates. The remaining five molecules of G3P are used to regenerate ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP), the initial CO2 acceptor molecule of the Calvin cycle. The regeneration of RuBP is crucial for the cycle to continue and for further CO2 fixation. So, after one complete turn of the Calvin cycle, there is a net gain of one molecule of G3P that can be used for carbohydrate synthesis, and five molecules of G3P are used to regenerate RuBP to keep the cycle running. It takes six turns of the Calvin cycle to produce one molecule of glucose, as six CO2 molecules are needed to synthesize one molecule of glucose.

questions.explanation: The Calvin cycle, also referred to as the C3 cycle, processes CO2 into carbohydrates in the stroma of chloroplasts. Each cycle fixes three CO2 molecules to produce six G3P molecules. However, five G3P molecules are recycled to regenerate RuBP, the molecule that captures CO2, thus allowing the cycle to proceed. As a result, only one G3P is a net gain for further carbohydrate synthesis. This is why the net gain of G3P after one Calvin cycle is one. The other options incorrectly assume a higher net gain due to a misunderstanding of the cycle's regeneration process.

questions.text: Two populations of the same species over time grow distant from one another. At what point will these two populations be considered different species?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: When the populations begin to eat different foods
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While dietary changes might indicate ecological differences, they do not define speciation. Speciation occurs when populations are reproductively isolated.

options.text: When there is a physical barrier, such as a river, between the two populations
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A physical barrier might lead to reproductive isolation over time but it is not in itself a defining factor for speciation unless it results in an inability to interbreed.

options.text: When the two populations have not been in contact with one another for two hundred years
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The duration of separation is not the key factor; the critical point is whether or not the populations can interbreed.

options.text: When they are no longer able to interbreed
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Speciation is confirmed when two populations become reproductively isolated, meaning they can no longer interbreed to produce viable offspring.

questions.explanation: Speciation is defined by the biological concept of species, which states that species are groups of interbreeding natural populations that are reproductively isolated from other such groups. Therefore, two populations become distinct species when they can no longer interbreed, not merely because of different diets, physical separations, or long periods of isolation. The correct option (D) highlights the importance of reproductive isolation, which is a key element in the process of speciation. Other options fail to address this crucial factor and are therefore incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following is the name of the modified endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T-tubules, or transverse tubules, are invaginations of the cell membrane in muscle cells. They play a crucial role in transmitting electrical impulses (action potentials) into the cell, ensuring synchronized muscle contraction.

A sarcomere is the basic structural unit of muscle fibers. It is the segment between two Z lines and consists of overlapping actin and myosin filaments. Contraction and relaxation of sarcomeres enable muscle movement.
There is no specific term "myoplasmic reticulum."

The modified endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells is called the "sarcoplasmic reticulum" (SR). The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a specialized type of endoplasmic reticulum (ER) that is specifically found in muscle cells, both in skeletal muscle and cardiac muscle. The primary function of the sarcoplasmic reticulum is to store and regulate calcium ions (Ca2+). Calcium plays a crucial role in muscle contraction. When a muscle is stimulated to contract, an action potential is generated, which leads to the release of calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum into the muscle cell's cytoplasm (sarcoplasm). The increased calcium concentration in the cytoplasm triggers a series of events that ultimately lead to muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is a membrane-bound structure found within muscle cells that is similar to the smooth endoplasmic reticulum in other cells. The main function of the SR is to store calcium ions (Ca2+).

questions.text: Carbohydrate catabolism is concerned with the fate of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adenosine Triphosphate, or ATP, is a molecule that carries energy within cells. It is often referred to as the "energy currency" of the cell because it provides the energy necessary for various cellular processes. ATP is composed of adenine (a nitrogenous base), ribose (a sugar), and three phosphate groups.

Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins. They are organic compounds composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sometimes sulfur. There are 20 standard amino acids that are commonly found in proteins.The human body cannot synthesize these amino acids, so they must be obtained from the diet.

Glucose is a simple sugar, a monosaccharide, and is a primary source of energy for cells. Glucose is broken down through cellular respiration to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the energy currency of cells.Glucose is broken down through cellular respiration to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the energy currency of cells.
This option is incorrect as the correct option here is Glucose only.

questions.explanation: Glucose is a simple sugar, a monosaccharide, and is a primary source of energy for cells. Glucose is broken down through cellular respiration to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the energy currency of cells.Glucose is broken down through cellular respiration to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the energy currency of cells.

questions.text: The scrotum is responsible for which of the following in male reproductive system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sperm synthesis occurs within the testicles, specifically in structures called seminiferous tubules, not in the scrotum. The scrotum houses the testicles but does not play a direct role in sperm synthesis.

Lubrication during sexual activity is mainly provided by the bulbourethral glands (Cowper's glands) and the prostate gland, not the scrotum. These glands produce fluids that contribute to the lubrication of the urethra and facilitate the movement of sperm.

The nourishment of sperm is primarily provided by the seminal vesicles, which produce a fluid rich in nutrients that enhances sperm survival and motility. The scrotum itself does not have a direct role in nourishing sperm.

This option is correct because sperm can survive at the temperature of 34°C, that is why they are outside the body. The scrotum regulates the temperature of the testis. Scrotum contracts in cold, exercise temperature while expands in high temperature.

questions.explanation: Thie option D is correct because sperm can survive at the temperature of 34°C, that is why they are outside the body. The scrotum regulates the temperature of the testis. Scrotum contracts in cold, exercise temperature while expands in high temperature.

questions.text: In Morgan's linkage experiment, when males and females of the F2 generation mate with each other and produce offspring, the percentage of recombinants was:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In Morgan's linkage experiment, when males and females of the F2 generation (second generation) mate with each other and produce offspring, the percentage of recombinants depends on the genetic distance between the linked genes. Morgan's experiments, conducted with fruit flies (Drosophila melanogaster), provided early evidence for the phenomenon of genetic linkage. He observed that certain traits (genes) tended to be inherited together more frequently than expected based on Mendelian inheritance patterns. These traits were located on the same chromosome and, therefore, showed linkage. During the F2 generation cross, if the linked genes are far apart on the same chromosome, then the percentage of recombinant offspring will be higher. This is because crossing over during meiosis can occur between the linked genes, leading to the exchange of genetic material and the formation of new combinations of alleles (recombinants). On the other hand, if the linked genes are very close together on the same chromosome, the percentage of recombinant offspring will be lower. In this case, crossing over is less likely to occur between the genes, and the majority of offspring will inherit the original combinations of alleles (parental types). To quantify the percentage of recombinant offspring in Morgan's linkage experiments, the data from the F2 generation cross would be compared to the expected ratios based on independent assortment (no linkage). The difference between the observed and expected ratios would give the percentage of recombinant offspring. In summary, the percentage of recombinant offspring in Morgan's linkage experiment will vary depending on the genetic distance between the linked genes on the chromosome. Closer genes will have fewer recombinant offspring, while genes farther apart will have a higher percentage of recombinants.

Morgan and his students have found that linked genes show varied recom­binations, some being more tightly linked than others. (i) In Drosophila, a crossing of yellow-bodied (Y) and white-eyed (W) females with brown-bodied (Y+) red-eyed (W+) males produced F1 to be brown-bodied red-eyed. On intercrossing of F1 progeny, Morgan observed that the two genes did not segregate independently of each other and, therefore, the F2 ratio deviated significantly from the expected 9: 3: 3: 1 ratio. He found 98.7% to be parental and only 1.3% recombinants. (ii) In a second cross in Drosophila between white-eyed and miniature-winged (mm) females with wild type or red-eyed normal winged (w+w+m+m+) males, all the F1 were found to be of wild type, i.e., red-eyed and normal winged. An F1 female fly was then test crossed with white-eyed and miniature winged male. 62.8% of the progeny was of parental types while 37.2% were recombinants.

questions.text: The human embryo remains enclosed in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The human embryo remains enclosed in the amniotic sac, which is filled with amniotic fluid. This sack provides:Protection: → Cushions the embryo from shocks.Temperature regulation: → Maintains a stable environment.Prevention of dehydration → Keeps the embryo moist.Movement space → Allows fetal movement for muscle development.
A temporary organ that provides nutrients and oxygen but does not enclose the embryo.
An outer membrane that forms part of the placenta but does not directly enclose the embryo.
Helps in waste removal and gas exchange but does not enclose the embryo.

questions.explanation: The human embryo remains enclosed in the amniotic sac, a fluid-filled membrane that provides protection and a stable environment for development. The sac is filled with amniotic fluid, which cushions the embryo against mechanical shocks, prevents dehydration, and allows free movement essential for proper growth. The amniotic sac, along with the placenta and umbilical cord, plays a crucial role in supporting the embryo throughout pregnancy by facilitating nutrient exchange and waste removal while safeguarding it from external harm.

questions.text: Cholesterol is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cholesterol is an animal sterol, and its chemical formula is C27H46O. It contains a total of 27 carbon atoms, 46 hydrogen atoms, and one oxygen atom.Cholesterol is a lipid molecule that plays a vital role in the structure and function of cell membranes in animals. It is an essential component of cell membranes, helping to maintain their fluidity and stability. Cholesterol is also a precursor for the synthesis of various hormones, including sex hormones (such as estrogen and testosterone) and adrenal hormones (such as cortisol and aldosterone).Additionally, cholesterol is involved in the production of bile acids, which aid in the digestion and absorption of fats in the intestines. While cholesterol is necessary for various physiological processes, high levels of cholesterol in the blood can be associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular diseases, such as atherosclerosis.Regarding the statement "5 methyl groups," cholesterol does indeed contain multiple methyl (CH3) groups in its structure, contributing to its chemical composition and functional properties.

questions.text: Which type of bonds are never formed when a substrate fits into the active site of an enzyme?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrogen bonds are weak interactions that can form between the substrate and the enzyme's active site, facilitating temporary binding.
Ionic interactions can occur when charged groups on the substrate and enzyme's active site attract each other, leading to a temporary association.
Hydrophobic interactions can occur between non-polar regions of the substrate and the enzyme, contributing to substrate binding.
Covalent linkages involve the formation of strong, permanent bonds and are not formed during the temporary interaction between the substrate and the enzyme's active site.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Covalent linkages because these bonds involve a strong and permanent connection between atoms, which is not typical during the transient interaction of a substrate with an enzyme's active site. Enzymes typically bind substrates through weaker interactions like hydrogen bonds, ionic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions, which facilitate the temporary nature of enzyme-substrate complexes. The other options—hydrogen bonds, ionic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions—are all types of interactions that can indeed occur as substrates fit into the active site, making them incorrect choices for this question.

questions.text: In some cases, the blastomere can produce a complete embryo so, the cleavage will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If a blastomere has the ability to produce a complete embryo on its own, it indicates that the early embryonic cells are totipotent. Totipotency means that the cell has the potential to differentiate into all cell types of an organism, including both embryonic and extraembryonic tissues. In such cases, the cleavage is typically spiral and indeterminate. Hence this option is incorrect.
In spiral cleavage, the cells divide along a diagonal axis, creating a spiral pattern of cells. This type of cleavage is determinate, meaning that each cell has a predetermined fate.

If a blastomere has the ability to produce a complete embryo on its own, it indicates that the early embryonic cells are totipotent. Totipotency means that the cell has the potential to differentiate into all cell types of an organism, including both embryonic and extraembryonic tissues. In such cases, the cleavage is typically spiral and indeterminate. Hence this option is incorrect.

In radial and indeterminate cleavage, the planes of cleavage are symmetrical to the polar axis and produce tiers of cells on top of each other and the fate of each blastomere is not pre-determined. In some anyone, blastomere can produce a complete embryo. Hence this option is correct.

questions.explanation: The answer is Radial and indeterminate. In radial cleavage, the cells divide along a vertical axis, creating a stack of cells. This type of cleavage is indeterminate, meaning that each cell has the potential to develop into a complete embryo. In spiral cleavage, the cells divide along a diagonal axis, creating a spiral pattern of cells. This type of cleavage is determinate, meaning that each cell has a predetermined fate.

questions.text: Primordial soup is a set of hypothetical conditions on ancient Earth first proposed by?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dmitri Ivanovsky is known for his foundational work in virology and the discovery of viruses, specifically in plants. He did not contribute to theories on the origin of life or the primordial soup hypothesis.
Dmitry Anuchin was an anthropologist and geographer, focusing on human cultures and archaeology. His work does not pertain to biochemical origins or the primordial soup theory.
Nikolay Shatsky was a geologist who contributed significantly to geological sciences, but not to theories about life's biochemical origins like the primordial soup hypothesis.

Alexander Oparin, a Soviet biochemist, is renowned for proposing the primordial soup hypothesis in 1924. He theorised that life began through gradual chemical reactions in early Earth's oceans, leading to complex organic molecules and eventually life. His work laid the groundwork for the study of abiogenesis.

questions.explanation: Alexander Oparin is credited with the primordial soup hypothesis, suggesting that life on Earth originated from a series of chemical reactions in a nutrient-rich 'soup' of organic compounds. This idea was groundbreaking in understanding abiogenesis. The other individuals listed — Dmitri Ivanovsky, Dmitry Anuchin, and Nikolay Shatsky — were influential in their respective fields of virology, anthropology, and geology, but did not propose theories on the origin of life or the primordial soup.

questions.text: Which of these are the largest animal viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus)—about 100–120 nm in diameter.HIV is a retrovirus that attacks the immune system, specifically CD4+ T cells, leading to progressive immune deficiency. It causes AIDS if left untreated and requires enzymes like reverse transcriptase, integrase, and protease for replication.

Poxvirus—about 200–400 nm in diameterPoxviruses are large, complex DNA viruses that replicate entirely in the cytoplasm of host cells. They include variola virus, which caused smallpox, and vaccinia virus, used in vaccines.

COVID-19 virus (SARS-CoV-2)—about 80–120 nm in diameter.SARS-CoV-2 is a coronavirus that emerged in 2019, causing the COVID-19 pandemic. It primarily infects the respiratory system and spreads through respiratory droplets and aerosols.

HBV (Hepatitis B virus)—about 42 nm in diameter.HBV is a small DNA virus that infects the liver, leading to acute or chronic hepatitis. Chronic infection can progress to liver cirrhosis or cancer, making it a major global health concern.

questions.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) measures about 100–120 nm in diameter. Poxvirus is the largest among them, with a size of about 200–400 nm. The COVID-19 virus (SARS-CoV-2) has a diameter of about 80–120 nm, while HBV (Hepatitis B virus) is the smallest, measuring only about 42 nm.

questions.text: The difference in the E.N values of the bonded atoms b/w two atoms is an index to of covalent bond:

options.explanation: Correct answer: Polar natureExplanation:Electronegativity (E.N) is the ability of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons towards itself in a chemical bond.The difference in electronegativity values between two bonded atoms determines the polarity of a covalent bond:1. If the difference is large:The electrons are unequally shared.The bond becomes polar covalent (one atom pulls electrons more strongly).Example: H-Cl (chlorine is more electronegative than hydrogen).2. If the difference is zero or very small:The electrons are equally shared.The bond is non-polar covalent.Example: Cl-Cl, O=O (same atoms, no difference in E.N).
Non-polar nature — occurs when E.N difference is negligible, not when there is a difference.
Strength — depends on bond length, bond order, not directly on E.N difference.
Nature — too general, not specific enough.

questions.explanation: The 'electronegativity of an atom' determines how strongly it attracts electrons to itself. The polarity of a bond is affected by the electronegativity values of the two atoms involved in that bond. A 'polar molecule' is a molecule in which one end of the molecule is slightly positive, while the other end is slightly negative. A diatomic molecule that consists of a polar covalent bond, such as HF, is a polar molecule. Hence, polar molecules result from differences in electronegativity of the atoms in the molecule.

questions.text: The equilibrium constant is always written as a ratio of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The equilibrium constant (K) is always written as a ratio of concentrations or pressures of the products to the concentrations or pressures of the reactants in a chemical reaction at equilibrium.

The equilibrium constant (K) is always written as a ratio of concentrations or pressures of the products to the concentrations or pressures of the reactants in a chemical reaction at equilibrium.For a general chemical reaction:aA + bB ⇌ cC + dDThe equilibrium constant expression, K, is given by:K = [C]^c [D]^d / [A]^a [B]^bwhere [C], [D], [A], and [B] represent the molar concentrations of the products C and D and the reactants A and B, respectively. The exponents (c, d, a, and b) are the stoichiometric coefficients of the balanced chemical equation.For reactions involving gases, the equilibrium constant expression can also be written using partial pressures (P) instead of concentrations:K = (P_C^c * P_D^d) / (P_A^a * P_B^b)In either case, the equilibrium constant provides a quantitative measure of the position of the chemical equilibrium and indicates whether the reaction favors the formation of products (K > 1) or the reactants (K < 1) at equilibrium. When the reaction is at equilibrium, the ratio of product concentrations or pressures to reactant concentrations or pressures remains constant and equal to the equilibrium constant, K.
The equilibrium constant (K) is always written as a ratio of concentrations or pressures of the products to the concentrations or pressures of the reactants in a chemical reaction at equilibrium.
This is incorrect as the correct one is available.

questions.explanation: At 'equilibrium', the rate of the forward and reverse reactions are equal. The equilibrium constant gives the ratio of the units (pressure or concentration) of the products to the reactants when the reaction is at equilibrium.

questions.text: Melting and boiling point of transition elements is higher due to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Binding energy refers to the energy required to disassemble a whole system into separate parts. In the context of transition metals, this refers to the energy needed to separate the atoms in the metal. Transition metals have higher binding energy due to the presence of more unpaired electrons in their d-orbitals, which can form more bonds and thus require more energy to break. Hence, their boiling and melting points are high

Transition elements are characterized by their partially filled d orbitals, which provide them with unique properties, including higher melting and boiling points. Here's why:1. Presence of Unpaired d Electrons: Transition elements have incompletely filled d orbitals in their electron configuration. These unpaired electrons contribute to the formation of strong metallic bonds between the atoms in the crystal lattice. Metallic bonds are formed due to the delocalization of electrons within the crystal structure, leading to strong attractive forces between the metal atoms.2. Increased Effective Nuclear Charge: The unpaired d electrons experience a higher effective nuclear charge (the net positive charge experienced by an electron in a multi-electron atom) compared to the fully filled d orbitals in non-transition elements. This increased effective nuclear charge enhances the attraction between the nucleus and the valence electrons, contributing to the higher melting and boiling points.3. Stronger London Dispersion Forces: Transition elements have more extensive electron clouds due to the presence of the d electrons. As a result, the London dispersion forces, which are attractive forces between temporary dipoles induced by fluctuations in electron distribution, are stronger in transition elements. These forces also contribute to the higher melting and boiling points.4. Increased Electron Density: The presence of unpaired d electrons results in higher electron density in the crystal lattice. This increased electron density leads to stronger electron-electron repulsions, which in turn contribute to the higher melting and boiling points.

Hardness is a measure of how resistant a material is to various kinds of permanent shape changes when a compressive force is applied. While hardness does correlate with high melting and boiling points, it's not a direct cause. However, transition metals are generally harder due to the same reasons that give them higher melting and boiling points (i.e., more bonding opportunities from unpaired d-orbital electrons).

This option suggests that all the above factors contribute to the higher melting and boiling points of transition metals. While A and B are directly responsible, C (hardness) is more of a correlated property rather than a cause. So, while all these factors are associated with the properties of transition metals, only A and B are the direct causes of higher melting and boiling points.

questions.explanation: Transition elements are characterized by their partially filled d orbitals, which provide them with unique properties, including higher melting and boiling points. Here's why:1. Presence of Unpaired d Electrons: Transition elements have incompletely filled d orbitals in their electron configuration. These unpaired electrons contribute to the formation of strong metallic bonds between the atoms in the crystal lattice. Metallic bonds are formed due to the delocalization of electrons within the crystal structure, leading to strong attractive forces between the metal atoms.2. This increased effective nuclear charge enhances the attraction between the nucleus and the valence electrons, contributing to the higher melting and boiling points.3. These forces also contribute to the higher melting and boiling points.4. Increased Electron Density: The presence of unpaired d electrons results in higher electron density in the crystal lattice. This increased electron density leads to stronger electron-electron repulsions, which in turn contribute to the higher melting and boiling points.

questions.text: The smallest part of the crystal lattice has all the properties of the entire crystal, this is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The unit cell is the smallest repeating structure in a crystal lattice that, when repeated in three dimensions, reproduces the entire crystal's pattern. It possesses the same properties as the overall crystal. Therefore, this option is not correct.

The smallest part of a crystal lattice that has all the properties of the entire crystal is called a "unit cell." A unit cell is a repeating structural unit that, when stacked together in three dimensions, forms the entire crystal lattice. The arrangement of atoms, ions, or molecules within the unit cell represents the crystal's basic building block, and this pattern repeats throughout the entire crystal structure. Properties such as symmetry, atomic arrangement, and density observed in the crystal are reflected in the unit cell. The unit cell's dimensions and angles are used to describe the crystal's lattice parameters, which define the overall structure and properties of the crystal. There are several types of unit cells, including simple cubic, body-centered cubic, face-centered cubic, and more complex unit cells in more complex crystal structures. The type of unit cell a crystal possesses depends on the arrangement of its constituent particles and the crystal system to which it belongs. By studying the unit cell and its properties, scientists and researchers can gain insights into the overall crystal structure and understand the material's physical, mechanical, and chemical behavior.

The unit cell is the smallest repeating structure in a crystal lattice that, when repeated in three dimensions, reproduces the entire crystal's pattern. It possesses the same properties as the overall crystal. Therefore, this option is not correct.

questions.text: Current in electrolysis is carried through?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In electrolysis, the current is carried by 'ions', rather than by electrons.
In electrolysis, the current is carried by 'ions', rather than by electrons.
In electrolysis, the current is carried by 'ions', rather than by electrons.

In electrolysis, the current is carried through a conducting medium, typically an electrolyte solution. An electrolyte is a substance that can conduct electricity when dissolved in a solvent or when molten. It contains ions that can move freely, allowing the flow of electric charge.During electrolysis, an external electric current is applied to the electrolyte through two electrodes, an anode, and a cathode. The anode is the positive electrode, and the cathode is the negative electrode. When the electric current is passed through the electrolyte, it causes a chemical reaction to occur at the electrodes.At the anode, oxidation takes place, and positively charged ions (cations) are attracted to the negatively charged electrode. At the cathode, reduction occurs, and negatively charged ions (anions) are attracted to the positively charged electrode. These ion movements carry the electric current through the electrolyte, facilitating the chemical reactions at the respective electrodes.The overall process of electrolysis is used to induce chemical changes, such as the decomposition of compounds or the deposition of metals from solutions. It finds applications in various fields, including metal extraction, electroplating, and various industrial processes.

questions.explanation: In electrolysis, the current is carried by 'ions', rather than by electrons.

questions.text: The substance that is attached to the enzyme at specific place and converted into product is called as?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The substance that is attached to the enzyme at a specific site and is converted into a product is called a substrate. Enzymes are biological catalysts that facilitate and accelerate chemical reactions, and they typically bind to substrates at their active sites where the conversion takes place. Therefore this option is not correct.

The substance that is attached to the enzyme at a specific site and is converted into a product is called a substrate. Therefore this option is not correct.

The substance that is attached to the enzyme at a specific site and is converted into a product is called a substrate. Enzymes are biological catalysts that facilitate and accelerate chemical reactions, and they typically bind to substrates at their active sites where the conversion takes place. Therefore this option is not correct.

The substance that is attached to the enzyme at a specific place and converted into a product is called a substrate. In enzyme-catalyzed reactions, the substrate is the molecule or molecules that the enzyme acts upon. Enzymes are biological catalysts that facilitate and accelerate chemical reactions in living organisms.The substrate binds to a region on the enzyme known as the active site, where the enzyme's catalytic activity occurs. This binding is highly specific, as enzymes are typically designed to recognize and bind to particular substrates based on their shape, charge, and other chemical properties. Once the substrate is bound to the enzyme's active site, the enzyme facilitates the conversion of the substrate into a product through chemical reactions, without being consumed or altered in the process. After the reaction is complete, the product is released, and the enzyme can go on to catalyze more reactions with other substrate molecules. This process allows enzymes to play a crucial role in regulating and driving the biochemical reactions necessary for life.

questions.explanation: The 'substrate' is the reactant in a chemical reaction when acted upon by an enzyme. Induced fit proposes that the initial interaction between enzyme and substrate is relatively weak, but that these weak interactions rapidly induce conformational changes in the enzyme that strengthen binding.

questions.text: How are carboxylic acids formed from alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Does Hydrolysis of Alcohols Occur? Technically, alcohols do not undergo "hydrolysis" in the usual sense.Hydrolysis is a reaction in which water breaks a bond in another molecule.Alcohols are already hydroxy compounds — so adding water doesn't break them further under normal conditions.

Reduction of Alcohols — ConceptReduction usually refers to gain of electrons or hydrogen or loss of oxygen.But since alcohols are already in a reduced state, reducing an alcohol further is possible only for specific types of alcohols, and it leads to alkanes.

Carboxylic acids can be formed from alcohols through a process called oxidation. The oxidation of alcohols involves the removal of hydrogen and addition of oxygen to the carbon atom that bears the hydroxyl group (-OH) in the alcohol molecule, resulting in the formation of a carboxylic acid functional group (-COOH).The specific method and reagents used for the oxidation of alcohols depend on the type of alcohol being oxidized. There are three main types of alcohols:1. Primary alcohols: These have the -OH group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to only one other carbon atom.2. Secondary alcohols: These have the -OH group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to two other carbon atoms.3. Tertiary alcohols: These have the -OH group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to three other carbon atoms.The oxidation of primary alcohols to carboxylic acids can be achieved using various oxidizing agents, such as potassium permanganate (KMnO4), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), or Jones reagent (chromic acid and sulfuric acid). The reaction typically involves heating the alcohol with the oxidizing agent.The general reaction for the oxidation of a primary alcohol to a carboxylic acid is as follows:R-CH2-OH (primary alcohol) + [O] → R-COOH (carboxylic acid)For secondary alcohols, the oxidation can yield ketones instead of carboxylic acids, and for tertiary alcohols, oxidation reactions are not feasible under mild conditions to produce carboxylic acids or ketones. Therefore, primary alcohols are the most common starting materials for the synthesis of carboxylic acids through alcohol oxidation.

What is Protonation of Alcohols?Protonation means addition of a proton (H⁺) to a molecule.When an alcohol (R–OH) is protonated, the oxygen atom of the –OH group accepts a proton because oxygen has lone pairs and is nucleophilic.This turns the –OH group into a better leaving group (H₂O).

questions.explanation: Primary alcohols can be 'oxidized' to form aldehydes and carboxylic acids; secondary alcohols can be oxidized to give ketones. Tertiary alcohols, in contrast, cannot be oxidized without breaking the molecule's C–C bonds.

questions.text: Energy for the endothermic reaction is given a _ value?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Energy for the endothermic reaction is given a positive value. In an endothermic reaction, the reactants have a lower energy level than the products, and energy is absorbed from the surroundings to drive the reaction. As a result, the change in enthalpy (∆H) for an endothermic reaction is positive, indicating that energy is being taken in or absorbed during the reaction.
Negative: A negative energy value indicates an exothermic reaction, where energy is released.
Zero: A zero energy value would mean no energy change, which is not the case for an endothermic reaction.
Neither positive nor negative: Endothermic reactions have a defined energy change, which is positive.

questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions are described as reactions involving an increase in energy levels of the product. So, as the substrate transitions into a product, the energy level increases, and this addition in energy is denoted by a '+' sign.

questions.text: Which alkyl halide gives SN2 reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is not correct.Primary alkyl halides typically undergo SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reactions. An SN2 reaction involves a one-step mechanism where a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon (carbon attached to the halide) from the backside, resulting in the displacement of the leaving group (halide ion) and inversion of the stereochemistry at the reaction center.

Primary alkyl halides typically undergo SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reactions. An SN2 reaction involves a one-step mechanism where a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon (carbon attached to the halide) from the backside, resulting in the displacement of the leaving group (halide ion) and inversion of the stereochemistry at the reaction center.The general reaction for an SN2 reaction is:Nu- (nucleophile) + R-X (alkyl halide) → R-Nu (product) + X- (halide ion)Here, R is the alkyl group, X is the halide (e.g., Cl, Br, I), and Nu- is the nucleophile. For the SN2 reaction to occur, the alkyl halide should be primary (the carbon attached to the halide is only bonded to one other carbon) because steric hindrance is minimal in primary alkyl halides, allowing the nucleophile to approach the backside of the carbon more easily.Secondary and tertiary alkyl halides do not typically undergo SN2 reactions due to increased steric hindrance around the reaction center, making it difficult for the nucleophile to attack from the backside. Instead, secondary and tertiary alkyl halides tend to undergo SN1 (substitution nucleophilic unimolecular) reactions, which proceed through a two-step mechanism involving carbocation intermediates.

This is not correct.Primary alkyl halides typically undergo SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reactions. An SN2 reaction involves a one-step mechanism where a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon (carbon attached to the halide) from the backside, resulting in the displacement of the leaving group (halide ion) and inversion of the stereochemistry at the reaction center.
This is incorrect as only option B is correct.

questions.explanation: The SN2 reaction mechanism involves the nucleophilic substitution reaction of the leaving group (which generally consists of halide groups or other electron-withdrawing groups) with a nucleophile in a given organic compound. The SN2 reaction is most favored by 'Primary Alkyl Halides'.

questions.text: In which of the following will water evaporate earlier?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: The rate of water evaporation depends on various factors such as the surface area exposed to air, temperature, humidity, and airflow. In general, water will evaporate more quickly in conditions where these factors promote evaporation. If we assume that the initial conditions (temperature, humidity, etc.) are the same for all containers, then the primary factor influencing the rate of evaporation would be the surface area exposed to air. Larger surface areas allow for more water molecules to be in contact with the air, promoting faster evaporation. A wide saucepan typically has a larger surface area compared to a cup, glass, or small bowl. Therefore, under similar conditions, water is likely to evaporate earlier from a saucepan than from the other containers.

questions.text: Free radical reactions take place in the presence of _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Heat is a form of energy that can be transferred from one object to another due to a temperature difference between them. It is a fundamental concept in thermodynamics, the branch of physics that deals with the study of energy and its transformations. Heat transfer occurs in three main ways: conduction, convection, and radiation.1. Conduction: Conduction is the transfer of heat through direct physical contact between two objects at different temperatures. In this process, energy is transferred from the hotter object to the colder one, resulting in a temperature equalization over time. For example, when you touch a hot metal spoon, heat is conducted from the spoon to your hand, making the spoon feel hot and your hand warm.2. Convection: Convection involves the transfer of heat through the movement of fluids (liquids or gases). When a fluid is heated, it becomes less dense and rises, while cooler fluid descends to replace it. This creates a circulating flow of the fluid, which facilitates heat transfer. Convection is responsible for phenomena like ocean currents, air circulation in the atmosphere, and the way heat is distributed within a pot of boiling water.3. Radiation: Radiation is the transfer of heat through electromagnetic waves without the need for any material medium. All objects emit thermal radiation based on their temperature. For example, the Sun emits heat to the Earth through radiation. Similarly, a hot stove emits infrared radiation, which we perceive as heat when we stand close to it.Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in an object. When two objects with different temperatures come into contact, the higher-temperature object transfers some of its kinetic energy (heat) to the lower-temperature object until both reach thermal equilibrium.Heat plays a crucial role in our daily lives and is involved in various processes, such as cooking, heating homes, generating electricity, and many chemical reactions. In thermodynamics, heat is often denoted by the symbol "Q." Its unit of measurement in the International System of Units (SI) is the joule (J), but in some practical applications, it is also measured in calories (cal) or British thermal units (BTU).

Free radical reactions take place in the presence of a source of energy that can break the covalent bonds in a molecule and initiate the formation of free radicals. Common sources of energy for initiating free radical reactions include heat, light (ultraviolet or visible), and other forms of radiation.The energy absorbed by a molecule can cause the homolytic cleavage (also known as homolysis) of a covalent bond, leading to the formation of two free radicals, each with an unpaired electron. These free radicals are highly reactive species and can initiate chain reactions by reacting with other molecules, creating more free radicals in the process.Initiation step (using heat, light, or radiation):AB → A• + B•Propagation steps:1. A• + B → AB2. A• + X → AX3. B• + Y → BYTermination steps:1. A• + A• → A-A (product)2. B• + B• → B-B (product)3. A• + B• → A-B (product)In these reactions, A and B are molecules, A• and B• are free radicals, and X and Y are atoms or molecules that can act as a reactant.Free radical reactions are essential in various chemical and biological processes and have implications in fields such as polymerization, combustion, atmospheric chemistry, and the biochemistry of living organisms.

A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed or permanently changed during the reaction. It facilitates the reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy, allowing the reactants to form products more rapidly. In other words, a catalyst lowers the energy barrier that the reactants must overcome to undergo the chemical transformation.The key points to understand about catalysts are:1. **Speeds up Reaction**: Catalysts accelerate chemical reactions by providing an alternative reaction pathway that requires less energy to reach the transition state, where the reactants can transform into products.2. **Not Consumed**: Catalysts themselves are not consumed in the reaction; they remain unchanged throughout the process. A small amount of catalyst can catalyze a large amount of reactants.3. **Does Not Alter Equilibrium**: A catalyst does not change the position of the equilibrium in a reversible reaction. It only affects the rate at which equilibrium is achieved, not the actual concentrations of the products and reactants at equilibrium.4. **Specific Action**: Catalysts are specific to particular reactions. They work for specific types of reactions based on their chemical properties and the mechanisms involved.5. **Regeneration**: At the end of the reaction, the catalyst remains in its original form and is available for reuse in subsequent reactions.The process of catalysis can be explained through the following steps:1. **Adsorption**: The reactant molecules are adsorbed or bound to the surface of the catalyst.2. **Activation**: This adsorption weakens the chemical bonds in the reactant molecules, making them more susceptible to reaction.3. **Reaction**: The weakened reactants react with each other or with other molecules to form products.4. **Desorption**: The products are desorbed or released from the catalyst's surface, leaving the catalyst unchanged.Catalysts are essential in various industrial processes, biological systems, and environmental reactions. They are widely used in the chemical industry to increase the efficiency of reactions and reduce energy requirements, ultimately leading to cost-effective and sustainable processes. Enzymes are a specific type of biological catalysts that play a crucial role in biochemical reactions in living organisms.

questions.text: Which type of bonds are broken during cracking?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: During cracking, a type of chemical reaction that breaks down larger hydrocarbon molecules into smaller ones, covalent bonds are broken. Cracking is a crucial process in the petroleum industry, where long-chain hydrocarbons from crude oil are converted into more valuable products such as gasoline, diesel, and other lighter hydrocarbons that are in higher demand.The covalent bonds in the long-chain hydrocarbon molecules are broken through the application of heat and/or the use of a catalyst. This process can occur through two main types of cracking:1. **Thermal Cracking**: In thermal cracking, heat is used to break the covalent bonds in the larger hydrocarbon molecules. This process typically occurs at high temperatures and under high pressure. It can be further divided into two subtypes: - **Visbreaking**: It involves the partial cracking of heavy residual oils to produce more valuable products. - **Coking**: It leads to the formation of coke (a solid carbon-rich material) along with lighter hydrocarbons.2. **Catalytic Cracking**: Catalytic cracking involves the use of a catalyst, typically a type of zeolite, to break the covalent bonds in the larger hydrocarbon molecules at lower temperatures. This process is commonly used in refineries to produce gasoline and other high-demand products.The cracking process is vital because it helps to balance the demand for different hydrocarbon products and maximize the efficiency of refining crude oil into valuable fuels and petrochemical feedstocks.
Cracking is breaking of C-C bond not C-H therefore it is incorrect.
Both can't be correct as only C-C bond breaks.
C-O bond is rarely present in organic compounds.

questions.explanation: Cracking is the process whereby complex organic molecules such as long-chain hydrocarbons are broken down into simpler molecules such as light hydrocarbons, by the breaking of 'carbon-carbon bonds' in the precursors.

questions.subject: Chemistry

The common name of the compound CH3(CH2)2CH2Cl is "n-butyl chloride."The compound is a halogen-substituted alkane (alkyl halide) and belongs to the alkyl chloride family. In the common naming system for alkyl halides, the prefix "n-" (normal) indicates that the carbon chain is linear (i.e., there are no branches). In this case, the carbon chain consists of four carbon atoms in a straight line, and there is a chlorine atom attached to one of the terminal carbon atoms. Therefore, it is named "n-butyl chloride."The IUPAC name for this compound would be 1-chlorobutane, which also indicates the presence of a chlorine atom attached to the first carbon of the butane chain. However, in common nomenclature, "n-butyl chloride" is more frequently used.

questions.explanation: CH3-CH2-CH2-CH2-Cl (straight chain)The common name of the compound is n-butanyl chloride as it has a 4 carbon atom chain with a chlorine attached to the last one. More specifically, it would be 1-butanyl chloride.

questions.text: During the course of an SN1 reaction, the intermediate species formed is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: During the course of an SN1 (Substitution Nucleophilic Unimolecular) reaction, the intermediate species formed is a carbocation (also known as a carbonium ion). The SN1 reaction mechanism involves a two-step process:1. Formation of the carbocation intermediate: The leaving group departs from the substrate, resulting in the formation of a positively charged carbon atom (carbocation). This step is often the rate-determining step of the reaction.2. Nucleophilic attack: A nucleophile (e.g., a negatively charged ion or a molecule with a lone pair of electrons) then reacts with the carbocation, filling the empty p orbital and displacing the leaving group. This leads to the substitution of the leaving group by the nucleophile.SN1 reactions are characterized by their dependence on the concentration of the substrate only (unimolecular), and the reaction rate is proportional to the concentration of the substrate. These reactions typically occur in the presence of weak nucleophiles and in polar protic solvents that can stabilize the carbocation intermediate.

questions.text: Which ion will have a maximum value of the heat of hydration?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The ion that will have the maximum value of the heat of hydration is the smaller and more highly charged ion. Heat of hydration refers to the energy released or absorbed when one mole of ions in the gaseous state combines with water molecules to form hydrated ions in an aqueous solution.When a small, highly charged ion comes into contact with water molecules, it can interact strongly with the water's dipole, resulting in a highly exothermic process where energy is released. On the other hand, larger ions or ions with lower charges have weaker interactions with water and generally have lower heat of hydration values.For example, let's compare the heat of hydration of two alkali metal ions: Li+ and Cs+. Lithium (Li) is smaller and has a higher charge (+1) compared to cesium (Cs), which is larger and has a charge of +1 as well. The heat of hydration for Li+ will be higher than that of Cs+ due to its smaller size and higher charge.So, in general, small, highly charged ions tend to have the highest values of heat of hydration.

questions.text: Which of the following method is used to prepare Ethylene glycol from ethene?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Dehydration of ethylene glycol does not result in formation of ethene.

Ethylene glycol is commonly prepared from ethene (ethylene) using the catalytic hydration method. In this process, ethene gas reacts with water in the presence of a catalyst to produce ethylene glycol.The balanced chemical equation for the preparation of ethylene glycol from ethene is as follows:C2H4 + H2O (in the presence of a catalyst) → HOCH2CH2OH (Ethylene glycol)The reaction typically takes place at elevated temperatures and pressures, and the catalyst used is usually a strong acid catalyst, such as phosphoric acid (H3PO4) or sulfuric acid (H2SO4).It's worth noting that ethylene glycol is an essential industrial chemical used primarily as an antifreeze agent, coolant, and in the production of polyester resins and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plastics.
Hydration of ethylene glycol does not result in formation of ethene.

questions.explanation: When ethene is subjected to 'hydroxylation' in the presence of cold and dilute alkaline potassium permanganate solution, the addition of hydroxide ions takes place across the double bond of ethene. It results in the formation of 'ethylene glycol'.

questions.text: Which of the following is a poor 'leaving' group in nucleophilic substitution reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is not correct.Cl- is small and has a stronger C-Cl bond. It's leaving ability is good.
This is not correct.Br- is medium-sized and has a moderate C-Br bond. It's leaving ability is very good.
This is not correct.I- is large and has the weakest C-I bond. It's leaving ability is excellent.

A poor "leaving" group in nucleophilic substitution reactions is an entity that is reluctant to depart from the molecule as a stable leaving species. Generally, good leaving groups are weak bases or stable ions, while poor leaving groups are strong bases or unstable ions.Among the given options, hydroxide ion (OH⁻) is a poor leaving group in nucleophilic substitution reactions. It is a strong base and is stable in its ionic form. However, when it acts as a leaving group, it necessitates the formation of a strong O-H bond, which is a highly unfavorable process. Therefore, reactions involving OH⁻ as a leaving group tend to be very slow and unfavorable.

questions.explanation: The weaker the base, the better the leaving group. Since OH- is the strongest base out of the options given, since the remaining options are Halogens and Halogens are acidic.

questions.text: By increasing which of the following factor polarizability increases?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Polarizability increases with increasing atomic size (or atomic radius). Polarizability refers to the ability of an atom or ion to undergo deformation of its electron cloud in response to an external electric field. Larger atoms have more loosely held electrons and a greater distance between the outermost electrons and the nucleus, which results in a more easily distorted electron cloud. As a result, larger atoms or ions have higher polarizability compared to smaller ones.In the periodic table, polarizability generally increases as you move down a group (vertically) and decreases as you move across a period (horizontally). This is because atoms get larger going down a group and smaller going across a period. As you move from top to bottom within a group, the atomic size increases, leading to higher polarizability. Conversely, moving from left to right along a period, the atomic size decreases, resulting in lower polarizability.So, among the factors mentioned in the options, increasing atomic size will increase polarizability.

The ionization energy is a measure of the capability of an element to enter into chemical reactions requiring ion formation or donation of electrons. It is also generally related to the nature of the chemical bonding in the compounds formed by the elements. See also binding energy; electron affinity.

Ionic radius is the distance from the nucleus of an ion up to which it has an influence on its electron cloud. Ions are formed when an atom loses or gains electrons. When an atom loses an electron it forms a cation and when it gains an electron it becomes an anion.

Hydration energy, also known as hydration enthalpy, can be defined as the amount of energy released when one mole of ions undergo hydration, which is a special case of solvation. It is a special case of dissolution of energy with the solvent being water

questions.explanation: Polarizability usually refers to the tendency of matter, when subjected to an electric field, to acquire an electric dipole moment in proportion to that applied field. Hence, increasing the size of the atom should increase its polarizability. Size of the atom can be increased by increasing the 'atomic radius'.

questions.text: What are the products of destructive distillation of coal?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Coal Tar: A dark, thick liquid with a pungent odour, coal tar contains a variety of chemical compounds, including aromatic hydrocarbons, phenols, and other organic substances. It has been used in various applications, such as medicine, road construction, and as a source of chemicals for the synthesis of other products.

Coke: This is a solid residue left behind after the volatile components of coal are driven off. It is a porous, carbon-rich material and is used as a fuel and as a reducing agent in the production of iron and steel.

Coal Gas: This is a mixture of gases, primarily methane (CH4), hydrogen (H2), carbon monoxide (CO), and a small amount of other hydrocarbons. It was historically used for heating, lighting, and powering early industrial processes.

Destructive distillation, also known as pyrolysis or carbonization, is a process that involves heating coal in the absence of air to break it down into various products. The primary products of destructive distillation of coal include:1. **Coal Gas**: This is a mixture of gases, primarily methane (CH4), hydrogen (H2), carbon monoxide (CO), and a small amount of other hydrocarbons. It was historically used for heating, lighting, and powering early industrial processes.2. **Coal Tar**: A dark, thick liquid with a pungent odor, coal tar contains a variety of chemical compounds, including aromatic hydrocarbons, phenols, and other organic substances. It has been used in various applications, such as medicine, road construction, and as a source of chemicals for the synthesis of other products.3. **Coal Oil or Coal Distillate**: This is a light oil fraction obtained during the distillation process, consisting of hydrocarbons and aromatic compounds.4. **Ammonia Liquor**: A liquid containing ammonia and various dissolved substances, which has applications in the chemical industry.5. **Coke**: This is a solid residue left behind after the volatile components of coal are driven off. It is a porous, carbon-rich material and is used as a fuel and as a reducing agent in the production of iron and steel.6. **Coal Char**: Also known as charred coal, it is a solid residue similar to coke but with a higher carbon content.The process of destructive distillation of coal has historically been important in producing a range of valuable products before the widespread use of petroleum-based alternatives. While it is not as prominent today due to environmental and economic considerations, it still has some niche applications in specialized industries.

questions.explanation: Destructive distillation is a chemical process in which decomposition is achieved by heating to a high temperature; the term generally applies to the processing of organic material in the absence of air or in the presence of limited amounts of oxygen. The process breaks up large molecules. 'Coke, coal gas, gaseous carbon, coal tar, ammonia liquor ,and coal oil' are examples of commercial products historically produced by the destructive distillation of coal.

questions.text: Which of the following condition is constant in Bomb calorimeter?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a Bomb calorimeter, the combustion reaction takes place in a strong sealed container (the “bomb”).Since the bomb is rigid and does not expand, the reaction occurs at constant volume .Therefore, it measures the change in internal energy (ΔU), not enthalpy (ΔH)

In a bomb calorimeter, the condition that is kept constant is the volume. A bomb calorimeter is a device used to measure the heat of combustion of a substance by igniting it in a high-pressure vessel (the "bomb") surrounded by water. The heat released during the combustion reaction is transferred to the surrounding water, and the temperature change of the water is measured to determine the heat of combustion.To ensure accurate and precise measurements, the volume of the bomb remains constant throughout the experiment. This is achieved by using a rigid, sealed vessel that does not allow any gas to escape during the combustion process. By keeping the volume constant, any change in temperature observed in the water can be directly attributed to the heat released during the combustion reaction, allowing for accurate heat measurement and subsequent calculations.

In a Bomb calorimeter, the combustion reaction takes place in a strong sealed container (the “bomb”).Since the bomb is rigid and does not expand, the reaction occurs at constant volume .Therefore, it measures the change in internal energy (ΔU), not enthalpy (ΔH).

In a Bomb calorimeter, the combustion reaction takes place in a strong sealed container (the “bomb”).Since the bomb is rigid and does not expand, the reaction occurs at constant volume .Therefore, it measures the change in internal energy (ΔU), not enthalpy (ΔH)

questions.explanation: A bomb calorimeter is a type of 'constant-volume calorimeter' used in measuring the heat of combustion of a particular reaction.

questions.text: The shielding effect is also called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.Zee effect: This is not a recognized term in chemistry.

The shielding effect is also called the "screening effect." In chemistry and physics, the shielding effect refers to the reduction in the net attractive force between the positively charged nucleus of an atom and its outermost electrons due to the presence of inner electron shells. Inner electron shells act as a shield, partially neutralizing the positive charge of the nucleus and reducing its influence on the outer electrons.The shielding effect plays a crucial role in determining the chemical and physical properties of atoms, particularly with respect to atomic size, ionization energy, and electron affinity. Elements with effective shielding experience weaker attraction between the nucleus and valence electrons, leading to larger atomic size and lower ionization energy compared to elements with less effective shielding.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect. Negative effect: This is too general and doesn't specifically relate to the shielding of nuclear charge.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.Reducing effect: While shielding does reduce the effective nuclear charge, "reducing effect" isn't the standard terminology for it.

questions.explanation: The shielding effect is the reduction in the effective nuclear charge on the electron cloud, due to differences in the attraction forces between electrons and the nucleus. The shielding effect is also known as the 'Screening Effect'. Hence, there is no difference between these two terms. They primarily mean the same thing.

questions.text: Carboxylic acids do not give the characteristics reaction of >C=O group because of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The polar nature of carboxylic acids enhances their solubility in polar solvents like water. However, it does not explain the unique reactivity of the carbonyl group in carboxylic acids compared to aldehydes and ketones. Therefore, this option is not correct.

While the attached alkyl group can influence the overall electronic environment of the carboxylic acid, it does not primarily account for the difference in reactivity of the carbonyl group. It is the interplay of resonance stabilization and the presence of other functional groups that plays a more significant role.

The resonance stabilization in carboxylic acids reduces the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon, making it less reactive than in aldehydes and ketones. This resonance effect is the primary reason for the absence of characteristic reactivity associated with the carbonyl group in these acids, making this option correct.

Although the symmetrical structure may influence certain physical properties of carboxylic acids, it does not fundamentally explain why these compounds do not exhibit the characteristic reactions typical of carbonyl groups in other organic compounds. Thus, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The carbonyl group of ketones & aldehydes is more. electrophilic than in carboxylic acids. This is because the lone pairs on the Oxygen atom attached to a Hydrogen atom in the CO2H are involved in 'resonance', thereby making the carbon atom less electrophilic.

questions.text: Which of the following is most soluble in water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Among the given options, **ethanoic acid** (also known as acetic acid) is the most soluble in water.The solubility of carboxylic acids in water generally decreases with an increase in the length of their hydrocarbon chain. Ethanoic acid has the shortest hydrocarbon chain among the options provided, making it the most soluble in water due to its ability to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules.As the hydrocarbon chain increases in length (pentanoic acid, hexanoic acid, and butanoic acid), the nonpolar hydrophobic part of the molecule becomes more dominant, reducing the overall solubility in the polar solvent (water). Therefore, ethanoic acid, with its small hydrocarbon chain and polar -COOH group, exhibits the highest solubility in water among the given carboxylic acids.
Smaller carboxylic acids (C1 to C5) are soluble in water, whereas larger carboxylic acids (C6 and above) are less soluble due to the increasing hydrophobic nature of the hydrocarbon chains.

questions.text: Which of the following is produced by heating of bones?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: **Gelatine** is produced by heating bones, cartilage, and connective tissues of animals. When bones are heated in water for an extended period, collagen, a fibrous protein found in these tissues, breaks down into gelatin. Gelatin is widely used in the food industry for its gelling, thickening, and stabilizing properties. It is commonly used in desserts, gummy candies, marshmallows, and various other food products. Additionally, gelatin is used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and photography.

questions.text: Different kinds of atoms of the same element are called isotopes having different _ but same _ properties.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect.Different kinds of atoms of the same element, which have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons (resulting in different masses), are called isotopes. Isotopes of the same element have different physical properties, such as different atomic masses and, in some cases, different nuclear stabilities. However, isotopes of the same element have the same chemical properties. This is because chemical properties are primarily determined by the number and arrangement of electrons, which is determined by the number of protons in the nucleus. Since isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons, they exhibit the same chemical behavior.

questions.text: The number of reacting molecules which change their concentration in a chemical change is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The number of reacting molecules that change their concentration in a chemical change is often referred to as the reaction order. The reaction order describes how the rate of a chemical reaction depends on the concentration of reactants. It can be zero, first, second, or even a combination of these orders, and it is determined experimentally. This option is not correct.

The number of reacting molecules that change their concentration in a chemical change is called the **reaction order**. In a chemical reaction, the reaction order indicates how the rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of reactants. It determines the mathematical relationship between the rate of the reaction and the concentrations of the reactants. The reaction order with respect to each reactant can be 0, 1, 2, or even fractional, and it is determined experimentally by analyzing the rate of reaction under different initial concentrations of the reactants.For example, a reaction with a first-order reaction order with respect to a particular reactant means that the rate of the reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of that reactant. A reaction with a second-order reaction order with respect to a reactant means that the rate of the reaction is proportional to the square of the concentration of that reactant. The overall reaction order is the sum of the individual reaction orders for each reactant in the rate equation. Understanding reaction orders is essential in studying reaction kinetics and determining the rate laws of chemical reactions.

The reaction order describes how the rate of a chemical reaction depends on the concentration of reactants. It can be zero, first, second, or even a combination of these orders, and it is determined experimentally. This option is not correct.

It can be zero, first, second, or even a combination of these orders, and it is determined experimentally. This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The 'order of a chemical reaction' can be defined as the sum of the power of concentration of reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of that chemical reaction. Reactions can be a first-order reaction, second-order reaction, pseudo-first-order reaction, etc. depending on the concentration of the reactants. The order of a reaction is an experimental value. It means it is an experimentally determined parameter. It can have a fractional value as well.

Lattices have important applications in various fields, such as geometry, number theory, graph theory, and the study of crystal structures in solid-state physics and materials science. questions.explanation: A 'lattice point' is a point at the intersection of two or more gridlines in a regularly spaced array of points, which is a point lattice. In the plane, point lattices can be constructed having unit cells in the shape of a square, rectangle, hexagon, and other shapes.

questions.text: The molecules of _ have dipole-dipole interaction.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Since ammonia is the correct answer, hence this option is incorrect.
Since ammonia is the correct answer, hence this option is incorrect.

The molecules of Ammonia (NH₃) have dipole-dipole interactions.Ammonia (NH₃) is a polar molecule, meaning it has a slightly positive charge on the hydrogen atoms and a slightly negative charge on the nitrogen atom. This polarity arises due to the difference in electronegativity between nitrogen (3.04) and hydrogen (2.20).The nitrogen atom in ammonia has a lone pair of electrons, which makes it slightly negative. The hydrogen atoms, being less electronegative, become slightly positive. This creates a permanent electric dipole moment in the NH₃ molecule.As a result, ammonia molecules can attract and interact with each other through dipole-dipole forces. The slightly positive hydrogen atoms of one molecule are attracted to the slightly negative nitrogen atom of another molecule, forming weak intermolecular bonds.Dipole-dipole interactions in ammonia are responsible for its:- Higher boiling point (compared to non-polar molecules of similar size)- Solubility in water (due to hydrogen bonding)- Ability to form hydrogen bonds with other moleculesThese interactions play a crucial role in the physical and chemical properties of ammonia.

Ammonia (NH3) is a polar molecule and exhibits dipole-dipole forces, while ethane (C2H6) and ethyl alcohol (C2H5OH) are nonpolar molecules. Dipole-dipole forces occur between polar molecules when the positive end of one molecule is attracted to the negative end of another molecule due to the separation of charges within the molecule.

questions.explanation: Dipole-dipole interactions occur when the partial charges formed within one molecule are attracted to an opposite partial charge in a nearby molecule. Polar molecules align so that the positive end of one molecule interacts with the negative end of another molecule. Even though 'ammonia' has hydrogen bonding, this is the best option here.

questions.text: The e/m values for the positive rays depends on _enclosed in a discharge tube.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The e/m (charge-to-mass) values for the positive rays depend on the **gas** enclosed in a discharge tube.Positive rays, also known as canal rays or anode rays, are produced when a high-voltage electric discharge is passed through a gas at low pressure in a discharge tube. The positive rays are formed by positive ions that are accelerated towards the cathode by the electric field. These positive ions have different charge-to-mass ratios (e/m values) depending on the type of gas present in the discharge tube.The e/m value is determined by measuring the deflection of the positive rays in a magnetic field perpendicular to the electric field. By varying the magnetic field strength, the path of the positive ions is curved, and the amount of deflection depends on the e/m value.The e/m values for positive rays helped physicists to identify and characterize different types of positive ions, which in turn led to a better understanding of atomic and subatomic structure in the early days of atomic and nuclear physics.

questions.text: How many chain isomers of Pentane is possible?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Total of 3 chain isomer of pentane are possible.Therefore it is incorrect.
Total of 3 chain isomer of pentane are possible.Therefore it is incorrect.

Pentane (C5H12) is an alkane with the molecular formula C5H12. Chain isomers are structural isomers that differ in the arrangement of carbon atoms in the main chain of the molecule. For pentane, there are three different chain isomers possible:1. **n-Pentane**: This is the straight-chain isomer, where all five carbon atoms are arranged in a continuous straight chain. CH3-CH2-CH2-CH2-CH32. **Isopentane (2-Methylbutane)**: In this isomer, the carbon chain has a branch with a methyl (CH3) group at the second carbon atom. CH3-CH(CH3)-CH2-CH33. **Neopentane (2,2-Dimethylpropane)**: In this isomer, the carbon chain has two methyl (CH3) groups attached to the second carbon atom, resulting in a branched structure. (CH3)3C-CH3These three chain isomers are the only possible arrangements of carbon atoms for pentane, and they represent all the different ways the five carbon atoms can be connected in a chain.
Total of 3 chain isomer of pentane are possible.Therefore it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Pentane has 'three isomers': n-pentane, iso-pentane, and neo-pentane.

questions.text: d-block elements are also called as _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect. d block elements are also called "outer transition elements" or "transition metals".The d-block of the periodic table consists of elements from group 3 to group 12, and they are located in the central part of the periodic table between the s-block (groups 1 and 2) and the p-block (groups 13 to 18). These elements are characterized by the presence of partially filled d orbitals in their electron configuration.

d block elements are also called "outer transition elements" or "transition metals".The d-block of the periodic table consists of elements from group 3 to group 12, and they are located in the central part of the periodic table between the s-block (groups 1 and 2) and the p-block (groups 13 to 18). These elements are characterized by the presence of partially filled d orbitals in their electron configuration.Transition elements are known for their unique properties, including:1. Variable oxidation states: Transition elements can exhibit multiple oxidation states due to the availability of partially filled d orbitals, allowing them to form various complex compounds.2. Colored compounds: Many transition metal compounds are brightly colored due to the absorption of certain wavelengths of light by their d electrons.3. Formation of complex ions: Transition metals can form complex ions by coordinating with ligands, which are molecules or ions that can donate electron pairs to the metal ion.4. Catalytic activity: Transition metals are often excellent catalysts in various chemical reactions due to their ability to participate in redox reactions.These properties make transition elements important in various industrial, biological, and technological applications.
